cards.back: Number of variables that differ between two minterms. ABC̄ vs ABC → HD=1 (only C differs).

cards.front: When can two minterms be merged?
cards.back: When HD = 1. The differing variable is eliminated: PX̄ + PX = P. HD > 1 → cannot directly merge.
Canonical: every term uses ALL variables (full minterms/maxterms). Non-canonical: simplified with fewer variables per term.
1. Boolean algebra (hand rules) 2. Karnaugh maps (graphical, ≤5 vars) 3. Quine-McCluskey (algorithmic, any size).
A + B. Proof: (A+Ā)(A+B) = 1·(A+B) = A+B. Uses the uniquely-Boolean distributive law.

cards.front: Gate count: canonical vs minimised majority voter?
cards.back: Canonical: 8 gates (3 NOT, 4 AND, 1 OR). Minimised: 4 gates (3 AND, 1 OR). 50% reduction.

cards.front: Why can you reuse terms (idempotent) for merging?
cards.back: A + A = A. Adding duplicate of any term doesn't change the function. Allows pairing one minterm with multiple neighbors.
HD = 3. All variables differ. No direct merging possible. Maximum HD for 3-variable terms.
Area ↓, Cost ↓, Power ↓, Speed ↑, Debuggability ↑. Fewer gates = better on every metric.

cards.front: How does K-map relate to Hamming distance?
cards.back: K-map arranges minterms so HD-1 neighbors are physically adjacent. Finding groups on the map = finding HD-1 pairs automatically.
